title: Biology   Biological factors can make emotion regulation harder. Lack of Skill

pages.text:
211
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Emotion Regulation Handout 4   (Emotion Regulation Worksheets 3, 16)
What Makes It Hard to Regulate Your Emotions
Biology

 Biological factors can make emotion regulation harder.
Lack of Skill

 You don’t know what to do to regulate your emotions.
Reinforcement of Emotional Behavior

 Your environment reinforces you when you are highly emotional.
Moodiness

 Your current mood controls what you do instead of your Wise Mind.

 You don’t really want to put in time and effort to regulate your emotions.
Emotional Overload

 High emotional arousal causes you to reach a skills breakdown point.
You can’t follow skills instructions or figure out what to do.
Emotion Myths

 Myths (e.g., mistaken beliefs) about emotions get in the way of your
ability to regulate emotions.

 Myths that emotions are bad or weak lead to avoiding emotions.

 Myths that extreme emotions are necessary or are part of who you are
keep you from trying to regulate your emotions.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Biology   Biological factors can make emotion regulation harder. Lack of Skill
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Emotion work starts with the body and access needs. Hunger, pain, oxygen, sleep attacks, MCAS reactions, trauma cues, and exhaustion count.
tiny_steps: Check body basics: breathing, pain, food/water, meds, temperature, sensory load.
Lower the demand by one size.
Choose soothe, solve, pause, or ask for help.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Allow anger and grief without turning them into shame.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not demand immediate calm during respiratory distress, pain flares, dissociation, or overload.
